worship.prayers: Poseidon was invoked as " Ennosigaios " ( Earth-Shaker ) when seeking protection from earthquakes and as "Soter" (Savior) for safe sea travel. Prayers were typically offered at the shore or from ship decks, with outstretched arms toward the sea. Before major voyages, captains sacrificed at his temples and promised greater offerings upon successful return. Coastal peoples prayed for protection from tsunamis and sea raiders, while horse-tamers and charioteers sought his blessing at hippodromes.
worship.sacred_sites: Poseidon was worshipped throughout the Greek world, especially in coastal cities and islands. His most famous temple stood at Cape Sounion , perched dramatically on cliffs overlooking the Aegean Sea, where sailors offered prayers for safe voyages. The sanctuary at Corinth (Isthmia) hosted the Isthmian Games in his honor. Other major cult centers included Helike (destroyed by earthquake and tsunami in 373 BCE, seen as divine punishment), Onchestos, Tainaron (with a legendary entrance to the underworld ), and temples throughout the Peloponnese.
worship.offerings: Sailors poured libations of wine into the sea before voyages and offered thanksgiving sacrifices upon safe return. Black bulls and stallions were particularly sacred offerings to Poseidon , sometimes drowned in the sea rather than burned on altars. Fishermen offered first catches, and coastal cities held regular processions to his shrines. Pine branches, sea salt, and water were used in purification rituals. After safe voyages, sailors dedicated votive anchors, ship models, and oars at his temples.
festivals: Isthmian Games: Held every two years at the Isthmus of Corinth in honor of Poseidon . One of the four pan-Hellenic games, athletes competed for crowns of pine (later wild celery). The games featured chariot racing, reflecting Poseidon 's association with horses, alongside athletic contests and musical competitions.
Poseidonia: Festivals celebrated throughout Greek cities, particularly in winter when seafaring was most dangerous. Sacrifices ensured calm seas for the coming sailing season.
Poseideion: The month named after Poseidon (roughly December), when rites were performed seeking his favor for the year ahead.

role: God of the Sea, Earthquakes, and Horses
archetype_description: Poseidon embodies the Water Deity archetype - master of the primordial seas, representing both life-giving sustenance and destructive chaos.

listDisplay.expandedContent: One of the three great sons of Kronos who divided the cosmos, Poseidon commands the vast oceans, shakes the earth with his mighty trident, and created the first horses. Known as the Earth-Shaker and Lord of the Seas, he embodies the dual nature of water itself: life-giving sustenance and terrifying destruction. Sailors, fishermen, and coastal peoples throughout the ancient world honored this tempestuous deity.

name: Poseidon
title: Greek - Poseidon

sections.title: Description
sections.content: One of the three great sons of Kronos who divided the cosmos, Poseidon commands the vast oceans, shakes the earth with his mighty trident, and created the first horses. Known as the Earth-Shaker and Lord of the Seas, he embodies the dual nature of water itself: life-giving sustenance and terrifying destruction. Sailors, fishermen, and coastal peoples throughout the ancient world honored this tempestuous deity.

description: Also known as 🔱 Poseidon. Associated with Petraios (of Rocks), Hippios (of Horses), Ennosigaios (Earth-Shaker). Known by the epithets: Petraios (of Rocks).
summary: One of the three great sons of Kronos who divided the cosmos, Poseidon commands the vast oceans, shakes the earth with his mighty trident, and created the first horses. Known as the Earth-Shaker and Lord of the Seas, he embodies the dual nature of water itself: life-giving sustenance and terrifying destruction. Sailors, fishermen, and coastal peoples throughout the ancient world honored this tempestuous deity.

worshipPractices.name: Sacrifices
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of bulls, horses, and other animals were made to Poseidon, often near the sea. Libations of wine and honey were also common.

worshipPractices.name: Prayers and Vows
worshipPractices.description: Sailors and fishermen would pray to Poseidon for safe voyages and bountiful catches, often making vows to him.

festivals.name: Poseidaea
festivals.description: Festivals held in honor of Poseidon, often involving athletic competitions and sacrifices.

festivals.name: Isthmian Games
festivals.description: Held at the Isthmus of Corinth, these games included athletic and musical contests dedicated to Poseidon.

cultural.modernLegacy: Poseidon remains a popular figure in modern culture, appearing in literature, film, and video games. His image is often used to represent the sea and maritime power. Themes of natural disasters and environmental awareness invoke his power.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Poseidon, son of Cronus and Rhea, was a major Olympian god in Greek mythology. Like his siblings, he was swallowed by Cronus shortly after birth to prevent a prophecy that a child would overthrow him. Zeus eventually rescued Poseidon and his other siblings. During the Titanomachy, the war between the Olympians and Titans, Poseidon received his trident from the Cyclopes, a powerful weapon that allowed him to control the seas and cause earthquakes. After the Olympian victory, Poseidon, Zeus, and Hades divided the cosmos, with Poseidon claiming dominion over the sea, earning him the title 'God of the Sea.' His Roman counterpart is Neptune.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Poseidon figures prominently in many Greek myths. He famously contested with Athena for patronage of Athens; he struck the Acropolis with his trident, creating a saltwater spring, but Athena offered an olive tree, winning the city. Poseidon played a significant role in the *Odyssey*, as he harbored a grudge against Odysseus for blinding his son, the Cyclops Polyphemus, causing Odysseus's long and arduous journey home. He also aided the Greeks in the Trojan War, though he later turned against them. Poseidon fathered many children, both mortal and divine, including the hero Theseus (in some accounts) and the monstrous Polyphemus. These tales showcase his immense power, volatile temper, and significant influence over both gods and mortals.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: The worship of Poseidon was widespread throughout ancient Greece, particularly in coastal regions and islands. Sailors, fishermen, and those who depended on the sea for their livelihood commonly venerated him, offering sacrifices and prayers for safe voyages and bountiful catches. Temples dedicated to Poseidon were often located near the sea, and festivals, such as the Poseidaea, were held in his honor. The Isthmian Games, held every two years at Corinth, were also dedicated to Poseidon. Offerings to Poseidon included bulls, horses, and other animals, as well as libations of wine and honey. His priests held a respected position in maritime communities, interpreting his will and overseeing rituals.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Poseidon's legacy extends far beyond ancient Greece. As Neptune, he remains a prominent figure in Roman mythology and art. His association with the sea has made him a popular symbol of maritime power and the forces of nature. In modern culture, Poseidon appears in numerous works of literature, film, and video games, often portrayed as a powerful and sometimes vengeful god. His image is used in nautical symbols, and his name is associated with ocean-related businesses and organizations. The ongoing exploration and study of the oceans continue to evoke the power and mystery of Poseidon, ensuring his enduring presence in the human imagination.

shortDescription: Greek god of the sea, earthquakes, and horses. A powerful and temperamental Olympian deity.
